core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing sapodilla are the same: full sun, free-draining soil, a warm and ideally frost-free spot, a named grafted tree, and a little patience, because it is a slow grower that rewards you for years once it settles in. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere sapodilla will grow.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Sapodilla is sold both as unnamed seedlings and as named, grafted cultivars, and the grafted trees are the ones worth seeking out, because they fruit far sooner and to a known type. Good named varieties include Alano, Prolific and Brown Sugar from the United States and the small dwarf-tree Makok from Thailand, while the trees most often grafted in Australia are Asian selections such as Krasuey, Sawo Manila and Ponderosa. Fruit size and texture vary by variety, from smooth and melting to slightly granular, so pick a named one and let the in-stock table above show what is available right now.

sections.heading: Seedling or grafted?
sections.body: A grafted, named sapodilla usually begins bearing in about two to four years, while a seedling takes far longer, commonly five to eight years and sometimes more, and a proportion of seedlings never crop well because of pollen problems. Seedlings also do not come true to the parent, so the fruit is a gamble. For a known fruit in a reasonable time, buy a grafted tree; sapodilla is normally propagated by side veneer or cleft grafting onto a sapodilla seedling rootstock. Seedlings still earn their keep as those hardy rootstocks, and as cheap shade or screen trees if you do not mind the wait.

sections.heading: Do you need two trees?
sections.body: Sapodilla is not simply self-fertile, and it pays to know that before you buy. Some named cultivars are self-incompatible, meaning their flowers need pollen from a different sapodilla seedling or variety to set fruit, while others fruit on their own but still crop more heavily when a second tree is nearby to cross-pollinate them; the tiny flowers are worked by small insects. If you only have room for one tree, choose a named, grafted variety known to fruit on its own, and if you have space, a second seedling or a different variety is the surest way to lift fruit set.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Sapodilla wants full sun and free-draining soil, and it is more forgiving than most tropical fruit trees: it grows in sand, loam or light clay, prefers a slightly acid to neutral soil of about pH 6 to 7, and once established it is notably drought-hardy, wind-firm and salt-tolerant, which makes it one of the better choices for exposed or seaside gardens. What it will not take is waterlogging, so avoid low spots where water sits and plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy. Give a young tree a warm, sheltered position, stake it for its first few years on windy sites, and mulch well while keeping the mulch back from the trunk. Container-grown trees go in best in the warm months (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Established sapodillas are very drought-tolerant and many older trees are never watered, but they crop better with steady moisture, so water young trees regularly to get them going and irrigate any bearing tree through dry spells. Sapodilla is a light feeder, best given a complete fertiliser little and often. A long-used Australian schedule gives about 100 grams of a high-potassium mix (such as a 10:2:17) per tree for each year of the tree's age, split across the warmer months, plus a yearly dressing of dolomite, while Florida growers use a balanced young-tree fertiliser of around 6:6:6 every couple of months. Potassium matters more than heavy nitrogen, since too much nitrogen drives leafy growth at the expense of fruit, and on poor soils a leaf or soil test is the best guide.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Telling when a sapodilla is ready to pick is the trickiest part of growing one, because the fruit gives few outward signs. It is mature, and ready to pick for ripening, when a light scratch of the skin shows tan or brown underneath rather than green, when the brown scurf rubs off easily to leave a duller skin, and when the stem parts cleanly without the cut weeping milky latex; on some varieties a small dry point at the base of the fruit drops off when it is ready. Picked at that stage, the hard fruit softens and sweetens off the tree over about four to ten days at room temperature. Never eat a sapodilla unripe: until it is soft it is gummy with latex, gritty and mouth-puckeringly astringent. A ripe one yields to gentle pressure like a soft pear and is intensely sweet. Scoop the flesh and discard the few hard, shiny black seeds, which carry a small hook and are not for swallowing.

sections.heading: Pruning and size
sections.body: Sapodilla is slow-growing but long-lived, and left alone in the tropics it becomes a large tree, so prune it after the main crop to hold it to a height you can net and pick. The wood is strong and the dense, glossy, evergreen canopy makes a good windbreak or shade tree. For a courtyard or a large pot, start with a dwarf selection such as Makok and keep it trimmed. Every part of the tree carries a milky latex (chicle, once the basis of chewing gum), so expect sticky sap whenever you cut it.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted sapodilla rather than an unnamed seedling: it fruits years sooner, comes true to type, and lets you match the variety to your space, a dwarf such as Makok for a pot or a full-size tree for an orchard. Check the label for the cultivar name, and remember that some cultivars fruit better with a second tree nearby. Sapodilla is genuinely hard to find in Australian nurseries and the grafted, named trees tend to sell out fast, which is exactly why the in-stock table above is worth watching.

faqs.q: What does a sapodilla taste like?
faqs.a: A ripe sapodilla is one of the sweetest of all fruits, with soft, smooth to faintly grainy flesh and a flavour often described as brown sugar or malt with notes of caramel and pear. It is rich and almost honeyed, low in acid, and at its best eaten chilled with a spoon.

The flesh is perfectly good to eat, but the few large black seeds are not: each has a small hook that could catch in the throat, so lift them out before serving, especially for children. They sit loosely in the flesh and come away easily.

faqs.q: Is sapodilla the same as chico, chiku or naseberry?
faqs.a: Yes. Sapodilla goes by many names around the world, including chico, chiku, sapota, naseberry, dilly and sawo, and they are all the one tree, Manilkara zapota. Do not confuse it with the unrelated jujube that is sometimes sold as chico.

faqs.q: Can I grow a sapodilla in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, especially a dwarf variety such as Makok. Sapodilla is slow-growing and takes well to a large container on a warm, sunny patio, which also lets you move it under cover in a cold snap. Use a free-draining mix, water through the warm months because pots dry out fast, feed regularly, and pot up every couple of years.

WA.intro: Growing sapodilla in Western Australia. WA can grow sapodilla, but in the warm tropical and subtropical north, not in the cool Mediterranean south-west, so in Perth it is a sheltered-microclimate gamble rather than a sure thing.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Sapodilla is a tropical tree, and the cool, wet winters of the south-west are its main obstacle in Western Australia. Mature trees take brief cold to about minus 3 degrees, so frost itself is not the whole story, but Perth and the south-west lack the steady warmth a sapodilla needs to flower and ripen well, so fruiting there is unreliable. The tree belongs in WA's warm north, where the heat and humidity suit it.

sections.heading: Where sapodilla grows in WA
sections.body: WA's tropical and subtropical fruit country is the place for sapodilla: Carnarvon on the Gascoyne coast, and Kununurra and the Ord River irrigation area in the east Kimberley, both grow a range of tropical fruit, with Broome warmer still. In Perth, a sapodilla is worth trying only in the hottest, most sheltered, frost-free corner of the garden.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly, not Queensland fruit fly, is the WA concern: it is established right across the state from Esperance to Carnarvon and lays into soft, ripening fruit, so a sapodilla needs the same baiting, bagging and monitoring as the rest of your backyard fruit. Scale and mealybugs are minor by comparison and are usually kept down by natural predators or an oil spray.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Western Australia's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List, and many eastern nurseries will not post trees here, so the in-stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can ship to WA. In practice that is mostly WA nurseries plus the few interstate sellers that meet the rules.

faqs.q: Can you grow a sapodilla in Perth?
faqs.a: You can try one in the warmest, most sheltered, frost-free spot in the garden, but Perth's cool, wet winters make flowering and fruiting unreliable. WA's dependable sapodilla country is the warm north around Carnarvon, Kununurra and Broome, not the south-west.

faqs.q: Which sapodilla varieties suit WA's north?
faqs.a: In the warm north almost any named, grafted variety should fruit; the Asian types such as Krasuey, Sawo Manila and Ponderosa suit hot, humid conditions, and a dwarf Makok does well in a large pot you can shelter. Start with grafted stock rather than a seedling.

QLD.intro: Growing sapodilla in Queensland. Queensland is the best of the eastern states for sapodilla: the tropical north grows it readily, and because the tree is hardier than most rare tropicals it fruits well down into the subtropical south-east too.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The warm, humid tropical north suits sapodilla, and unlike fussier rare tropical fruits the tree also copes with strong wind, salt air and both wet and drier districts, so it fruits along the coast and on the tablelands alike. Flowering is spread over a long season with several flushes a year, which is why a healthy tree can carry fruit at almost any time rather than in one short burst.

sections.heading: Where sapodilla grows in QLD
sections.body: The far north is the heartland. The wet tropics coast from around Ingham and Innisfail up through Babinda, Cairns and Mossman, and the Atherton Tableland and Mareeba behind them, is where most of Queensland's rare tropical fruit is grown, and sapodilla also fruits in warmer central and southern districts. Across the border the Northern Territory around Darwin grows the most, with sapodilla picked there in every month of the year.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Sapodilla flowers mainly from November to February in the north, with peak fruit set in January and February, and each fruit then takes about seven to nine months to mature, so the main northern harvest falls around September to November. Because the tree also flowers in lighter flushes through much of the year, the odd fruit ripens well outside that window.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Sapodilla is largely trouble-free, but two pests matter in Queensland. Queensland fruit fly will sting the ripening fruit, and sapodilla is on its commercial host list, so bag fruit or bait and monitor in fruit-fly country. Fruit-spotting bug attacks many exotic tropical tree crops and can mark or drop young fruit. Scale and mealybugs, and the sooty mould that follows them, are minor and usually handled by predators or an oil spray.

faqs.q: Which sapodilla varieties grow best in Queensland?
faqs.a: In the tropical north the Asian selections suit the climate, so look for grafted Krasuey, Sawo Manila or Ponderosa, along with dependable types like Alano and Prolific. Any named, grafted tree will fruit sooner and more truly than a seedling.

faqs.q: Can you grow sapodilla in Brisbane and south-east Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes. Sapodilla is one of the hardier tropical fruit trees, and an established one shrugs off the light frosts that sometimes reach the subtropical south-east, so it crops in Brisbane and the warmer inland where mangosteen or rambutan would fail. Give it the sunniest, most sheltered spot and protect it through its first winter or two.

NSW.intro: Growing sapodilla in New South Wales. NSW is the cool southern edge of sapodilla country: the warm, frost-free far north coast can ripen it, but it becomes a sheltered-garden gamble further south.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Sapodilla is a tropical to warm-subtropical tree, and in New South Wales it needs the frost-free far north coast, because young trees are killed by a frost of about minus 1 degree and even established trees only take brief cold to around minus 3 degrees. The humid coastal air also lifts the risk of fruit-fly attack and sooty mould, and inland or more southern districts are simply too cold and frosty for a dependable crop.

sections.heading: Where sapodilla grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers, the warm coastal country of the Tweed, Brunswick, Richmond and Clarence valleys around Byron Bay, Lismore and Grafton, is the only part of New South Wales reliably warm enough. South of there sapodilla is a backyard experiment for the mildest, most sheltered coastal pockets rather than a tree you can count on.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Sapodilla ripens more slowly in the cooler subtropics than in the tropical north, so on the New South Wales coast expect the crop to run later and to bunch into the warmer months rather than spreading across the whole year. A warm, sheltered position helps the fruit fill and sweeten before the cool weather arrives.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic along the warm New South Wales coast and will sting ripening sapodilla, so it is the main thing to manage; bag the fruit or bait and monitor through the season. Fruit-spotting bug, scale and mealybugs are the other things to watch, and good air movement helps keep sooty mould down in the coastal humidity.

faqs.q: Which sapodilla varieties suit the northern NSW coast?
faqs.a: On the warm far-north coast, choose a named, grafted tree and give it the most sheltered, frost-free spot you have. Reliable croppers such as Alano and Prolific, or Asian types like Sawo Manila, are good starting points, and a seedling is a poor bet this far south.

faqs.q: Can you grow sapodilla in Sydney or southern NSW?
faqs.a: It is a gamble. Sydney and the south are cooler and more frost-prone than sapodilla likes, so treat it as a warm-microclimate experiment: a grafted tree or a potted dwarf against a hot, north-facing wall, protected from frost, with any fruit a bonus.

VIC.intro: Growing sapodilla in Victoria. Sapodilla is not a Victorian crop: the state is far too cold and frosty for a tropical tree to flower and fruit outdoors, so here it is a glasshouse or potted curiosity rather than a backyard fruit tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Sapodilla needs tropical to subtropical warmth and will not take a hard frost, with young trees killed at about minus 1 degree. Victoria's winters are cool temperate: Melbourne averages overnight minimums near 6 degrees through winter with ground frosts common, and it is colder and frostier again inland and in the ranges. A sapodilla cannot get the heat it needs to flower and ripen here, and Australia's sapodilla country lies far to the north.

sections.heading: Growing sapodilla in Victoria anyway
sections.body: If you want to try, treat sapodilla as a container plant: a dwarf variety such as Makok in a large pot, kept in the warmest, most sheltered corner of the garden or in a glasshouse or conservatory, and moved under cover for winter. Expect foliage far more often than fruit. For a crop you can count on, a hardier subtropical fruit is the wiser choice, and sapodilla is best bought as fruit from the northern harvest.

faqs.q: Is it worth planting a sapodilla outdoors in Victoria?
faqs.a: Not as an outdoor fruit tree. Victoria is too cold and too frosty for a tropical sapodilla, which is killed by the frosts that are normal across much of the state in winter. A dwarf tree in a large pot, moved into a glasshouse or sheltered spot for the cold months, may survive but will rarely fruit.

faqs.q: Why won't a sapodilla fruit in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Sapodilla needs a long, warm, frost-free season to flower and ripen its slow-maturing fruit, and Melbourne's cool temperate climate does not provide it. The tree may live on in a pot or glasshouse, but the climate will not let it set and ripen a crop reliably.
